html:
XL

So I went to London Bridge, and again took my station on the spot by the booth where I had stood on the former occasion. The booth, however, was empty; neither the apple-woman nor her stall were to be seen. I looked over the balustrade upon the river; the tide was now, as before, rolling beneath the arch with frightful impetuosity. As I gazed upon the eddies of the whirlpool, I thought within myself how soon human life would become extinct there; a plunge, a convulsive flounder, and all would be over. When I last stood over that abyss I had felt a kind of impulseвБ†вАФa fascination: I had resisted itвБ†вАФI did not plunge into it. At present I felt a kind of impulse to plunge; but the impulse was of a different kind; it proceeded from a loathing of life. I looked wistfully at the eddiesвБ†вАФwhat had I to live for?вБ†вАФwhat, indeed! I thought of Brandt and Struensee, and Yeoman PatchвБ†вАФshould I yield to the impulseвБ†вАФwhy not? My eyes were fixed on the eddies. All of a sudden I shuddered; I thought I saw heads in the pool; human bodies wallowing confusedly; eyes turned up to heaven with hopeless horror; was that water, orвБ†вАФWhere was the impulse now? I raised my eyes from the pool, I looked no more upon itвБ†вАФI looked forward, far down the stream in the distance. вАЬHa! what is that? I thought I saw a kind of Fata Morgana, green meadows, waving groves, a rustic home; but in the far distanceвБ†вАФI staredвБ†вАФI staredвБ†вАФa Fata MorganaвБ†вАФit was goneвБ†вАФвАЭ

I left the balustrade and walked to the farther end of the bridge, where I stood for some time contemplating the crowd; I then passed over to the other side with the intention of returning home; just halfway over the bridge, in a booth immediately opposite the one in which I had formerly beheld her, sat my friend, the old apple-woman, huddled up behind her stall.

вАЬWell, mother,вАЭ said I, вАЬhow are you?вАЭ The old woman lifted her head with a startled look.

вАЬDonвАЩt you know me?вАЭ said I.

вАЬYes, I think I do. Ah, yes,вАЭ said she, as her features beamed with recollection, вАЬI know you, dear; you are the young lad that gave me the tanner. Well, child, got anything to sell?вАЭ

вАЬNothing at all,вАЭ said I.

вАЬBad luck?вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said I, вАЬbad enough, and ill usage.вАЭ

вАЬAh, I suppose they caught ye; well, child, never mind, better luck next time; I am glad to see you.вАЭ

вАЬThank you,вАЭ said I, sitting down on the stone bench; вАЬI thought you had left the bridgeвБ†вАФwhy have you changed your side?вАЭ

The old woman shook.

вАЬWhat is the matter with you,вАЭ said I, вАЬare you ill?вАЭ

вАЬNo, child, no; onlyвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬOnly what? Any bad news of your son?вАЭ

вАЬNo child, no; nothing about my son. Only low, childвБ†вАФevery heart has its bitters.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs true,вАЭ said I; вАЬwell, I donвАЩt want to know your sorrows; come, whereвАЩs the book?вАЭ

The apple-woman shook more violently than before, bent herself down, and drew her cloak more closely about her than before. вАЬBook, child, what book?вАЭ

вАЬWhy, blessed Mary, to be sure.вАЭ

вАЬOh, that; I haвАЩnвАЩt got it, childвБ†вАФI have lost it, have left it at home.вАЭ

вАЬLost it,вАЭ said I; вАЬleft it at homeвБ†вАФwhat do you mean? Come, let me have it.вАЭ

вАЬI haвАЩnвАЩt got it, child.вАЭ

вАЬI believe you have got it under your cloak.вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt tell anyone, dear; donвАЩtвБ†вАФdonвАЩt,вАЭ and the apple-woman burst into tears.

вАЬWhatвАЩs the matter with you?вАЭ said I, staring at her.

вАЬYou want to take my book from me?вАЭ

вАЬNot I, I care nothing about it; keep it, if you like, only tell me whatвАЩs the matter?вАЭ

вАЬWhy, all about that book.вАЭ

вАЬThe book?вАЭ

вАЬYes, they wanted to take it from me.вАЭ

вАЬWho did?вАЭ

вАЬWhy, some wicked boys. IвАЩll tell you all about it. Eight or ten days ago, I sat behind my stall, reading my book; all of a sudden I felt it snatched from my hand; up I started, and see three rascals of boys grinning at me; one of them held the book in his hand. вАШWhat book is this?вАЩ said he, grinning at it. вАШWhat do you want with my book?вАЩ said I, clutching at it over my stall, вАШgive me my book.вАЩ вАШWhat do you want a book for?вАЩ said he, holding it back; вАШI have a good mind to fling it into the Thames.вАЩ вАШGive me my book,вАЩ I shrieked; and, snatching at it, I fell over my stall, and all my fruit was scattered about. Off ran the boysвБ†вАФoff ran the rascal with my book. Oh dear, I thought I should have died; up I got, however, and ran after them as well as I could. I thought of my fruit; but I thought more of my book. I left my fruit and ran after my book. вАШMy book! my book!вАЩ I shrieked, вАШmurder! theft! robbery!вАЩ I was near being crushed under the wheels of a cart; but I didnвАЩt careвБ†вАФI followed the rascals. вАШStop them! stop them!вАЩ I ran nearly as fast as theyвБ†вАФthey couldnвАЩt run very fast on account of the crowd. At last someone stopped the rascal, whereupon he turned round, and flinging the book at me, it fell into the mud; well, I picked it up and kissed it, all muddy as it was. вАШHas he robbed you?вАЩ said the man. вАШRobbed me, indeed; why, he had got my book.вАЩ вАШOh, your book,вАЩ said the man, and laughed, and let the rascal go. Ah, he might laugh, butвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬWell, go on.вАЭ

вАЬMy heart beats so. Well, I went back to my booth and picked up my stall and my fruits, what I could find of them. I couldnвАЩt keep my stall for two days, I got such a fright, and when I got round I couldnвАЩt bide the booth where the thing had happened, so I came over to the other side. Oh, the rascals, if I could but see them hanged.вАЭ

вАЬFor what.вАЭ

вАЬWhy for stealing my book.вАЭ

вАЬI thought you didnвАЩt dislike stealing, that you were ready to buy thingsвБ†вАФthere was your son, you knowвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYes, to be sure.вАЭ

вАЬHe took things.вАЭ

вАЬTo be sure he did.вАЭ

вАЬBut you donвАЩt like a thing of yours to be taken.вАЭ

вАЬNo, thatвАЩs quite a different thing; whatвАЩs stealing handkerchiefs, and that kind of thing, to do with taking my book; thereвАЩs a wide differenceвБ†вАФdonвАЩt you see?вАЭ

вАЬYes, I see.вАЭ

вАЬDo you, dear? well, bless your heart, IвАЩm glad you do. Would you like to look at the book?вАЭ

вАЬWell, I think I should.вАЭ

вАЬHonour bright?вАЭ said the apple-woman, looking me in the eyes.

вАЬHonour bright,вАЭ said I, looking the apple-woman in the eyes.

вАЬWell then, dear, here it is,вАЭ said she, taking it from under her cloak; вАЬread it as long as you like, only get a little farther into the booth. DonвАЩt sit so near the edgeвБ†вАФyou mightвБ†вАФвАЭ

I went deep into the booth, and the apple-woman, bringing her chair round, almost confronted me. I commenced reading the book, and was soon engrossed by it; hours passed away, once or twice I lifted up my eyes, the apple-woman was still confronting me: at last my eyes began to ache, whereupon I returned the book to the apple-woman, and giving her another tanner, walked away.
